items.question_text: What is the mission of the traffic management system?

choices.text: Balance air traffic demand with system capacity so the NAS is used as efficiently as possible.
Reduce traffic volume whenever demand increases, even if capacity is available.
Assign miles-in-trail restrictions as the default response to any imbalance.
Leave demand balancing to traffic managers alone rather than the operating controllers.

items.explanation: The traffic management mission is to balance demand with capacity for the most efficient use of the NAS.

items.situation: Traffic demand is rising. A supervisor wants to default to a miles-in-trail program even though TBFM tools are available and functioning, arguing that MIT is simpler and that traffic management is mostly about slowing traffic down.
items.para_context: Use traffic management to balance demand with capacity. When feasible, use TBFM in preference to miles-in-trail initiatives, and remember that operating controllers are part of that mission rather than outside it.

choices.text: Use TBFM to the maximum extent feasible and manage the flow to balance demand with capacity rather than defaulting to MIT.
Default to MIT whenever demand rises because it is the preferred initiative whenever both tools are available.
Leave the decision entirely to traffic management personnel because operating controllers are not part of the traffic management mission.
Treat traffic management as a pure slowdown program and ignore available TBFM scheduling tools.

items.explanation: TBFM is the preferred tool when feasible, and traffic management is meant to balance demand with capacity rather than simply slow traffic indiscriminately.

items.question_text: What must the STMCIC ensure happens at least once during the day and evening shifts?

choices.text: An operational briefing with the required participants that covers items such as weather, staffing, equipment, runways, AAR, metering use, and current or anticipated TMIs.
A briefing only when traffic demand is already producing delays across multiple facilities.
A runway-status briefing for tower personnel only, with traffic management topics handled separately if needed.
A written briefing from TMU only, without requiring direct participation from operations supervision.

items.explanation: The STMCIC must ensure an operational briefing is conducted at least once during the day and evening shifts, with the required participants and core operational topics.

items.situation: A day-shift STMCIC says there is no need for the required operational briefing because everyone can read the weather and runway status separately, and metering changes can wait until congestion has already developed.
items.para_context: The STMCIC is responsible for making sure the required operational briefing happens and for ensuring metering operations and traffic-management initiatives are carried out and adjusted in a timely way. Operations supervision and air traffic personnel keep TMU and affected sectors apprised and coordinate changes as conditions evolve.

choices.text: Conduct the required operational briefing and make sure metering operations or TMIs are coordinated and implemented in a timely manner.
Skip the briefing because informal updates during the shift satisfy the requirement.
Wait for congestion to develop before sharing staffing, runway, equipment, or AAR information with the relevant participants.
Leave all metering changes to individual sectors because operations supervision does not need to coordinate them proactively.

items.explanation: The required briefing cannot be skipped, and metering operations or TMIs are supposed to be coordinated and implemented promptly rather than only after delay has already materialized.

items.question_text: During metering, how closely should personnel comply with TBFM-generated metering times unless an authorized exception applies?

choices.text: Within plus or minus 1 minute.
Within plus or minus 3 minutes.
Within plus or minus 5 miles if the time target cannot be met exactly.
As closely as practical, with no defined compliance tolerance.

items.explanation: The standard expectation during metering is compliance with generated metering times within plus or minus 1 minute, unless a valid authorized exception applies.

items.situation: During metering, a sector sees large jumps in the TBFM delay countdown timer for a pair of aircraft. Another controller wants to stop displaying the schedule information and ignore the metering times entirely, arguing that once the countdown becomes unstable there is no need to coordinate with TMU or adjacent sectors.
items.para_context: Keep TBFM schedule information displayed and comply with generated metering times as closely as required. If unstable countdown behavior makes that impractical for specific aircraft, supporting TMIs such as MIT or MINIT may be used until stability resumes, and coordination is required when compliance is not possible.

choices.text: Keep the schedule information displayed, use MIT or MINIT for the affected aircraft if needed, and coordinate with supervision, traffic management, and adjacent sectors when compliance cannot be achieved.
Stop using the schedule information entirely once the countdown becomes unstable, because coordination is no longer required after that point.
Ignore the plus-or-minus-1-minute standard during all metering periods and use any spacing that seems workable locally.
Replace TBFM metering with MIT or MINIT across the whole stream whenever any single aircraft shows countdown instability.

items.explanation: Schedule information stays in use, unstable aircraft can be supported with MIT or MINIT as needed, and coordination is required when the normal time-compliance standard cannot be met.
